Dean: So before we narrow down exactly what you're going to teach, right ... We already went
through understanding the high-level philosophy of the story that makes you the
authority, it gets them engaged, it gets them inspired, then you're going to teach them.
That's what we're going to go through now, is what exactly will you teach them so it's in
alignment with you and your perfect avatar? What I want to do now is share an example
of a story that I use that sets the tone.
So I asked you earlier about exposing your super power. What is some of the things your
competitors would say about you, why you're winning? What are the things that you
didn't even realize you do differently than everybody else? What are the things you love
to share, love to do? You wouldn't outsource it to anyone. And then, what's your hero's
journey? That was all strategic. Then we went from that to understanding who your
ideal client would be. You want the right people in those virtual seats or those real
seats. We talked about the power of the story, right? The story sets that intention. The
story is part of the dance.
So let me share with you, as if you were sitting in one of my masterminds and I was
talking about one of my unique abilities, one of my super powers. It's that I obsess on
measuring every piece of my marketing. I'm always trying to be the control. You'll learn
more about this when we get to the marketing section. If you're in marketing, you
already get it. So if I have a winning video that's pushing people to a winning sales pitch
that sells my book, I'm always measuring how much it costs to get that click, how many
people converted over to go to the page, how many people actually bought the book,
how many people bought more of our products and courses? I measure all that
obsessively.
So now, I shared with you, and again I'm just going to talk frank. I shared with you that
sometimes people inspire too much, but don't teach. A lot of personal growth videos do
that, right? I shared how other people get too tactical and you overwhelm or bore them
to death. So let's say I'm teaching in Tony and I's highest level mastermind, and I'm
teaching about measuring and tracking each part of your marketing. Now, I could start
by literally putting up a slide of my marketing KPIs and say, "This is how many people hit
the page. This is how much it costs. This is how they convert. This is what you have to
track. This is what you have to model. You always have to beat the control." That's a
part I'll get into, I'll show them the measuring and the tracking, at least through my
eyes. Someone else details, but this is what I need to look at. For sure, I'm going to
teach that. But that's the teach part.
So I could start that, and you'd be like, "Oh, Dean's done well, and we should model
this." But rather, I would start with a story. I'm going to give you one. I had the chance
to go to a mastermind in Idaho that was incredible. My friend Randy Garn owns a trout
ranch. Now [inaudible] never heard of a trout ranch, it's where this amazing stream
comes off the Idaho mountain and snakes all through his property, and they manage the
trout that live there. It's an unbelievable place to fish. And the most successful people
all around the world fly in and go there.

Well, I went there with an amazing group of people, from Brendon Burchard and Trent
Shelton, Russell Brunson, Jeff Walker, Lewis Howes, Anthony Trucks, Dave Hollis, and
Craig Clemons. Now, you might not know any of them, you might know all of them or
somewhere in the middle, but these are all high-achieving entrepreneurs. Three of
those people played in the NFL. So all competitive, all very successful, all good people.
And we're masterminding, our brains are ... I mean, all sharing our biggest
breakthroughs that masterminds are my life. And remember, when I say the word
mastermind, I also mean big groups, small group training, workshops, virtual, online, all
of it under the same umbrella.
I'm telling you this for a reason, because my friend Randy Garn who owns the trout
ranch, knows how competitive we are, and he set up a contest for fly fishing. Now,
here's the cool part. There were six different stations as the stream winds down through
their property. There's six different places where you can stand and fish. When they
blew this magical whistle, I didn't even know where it came from, or this bell, you got up
from where you were fishing, not that you were sitting down, and you went down to the
next section. They gave everybody a scorecard. There was five or six different kinds of
trout, so you got points for catching different variety. And then, of course, you got
points for the biggest.
So, you know, entrepreneurs, energetic, enthusiastic, and competitive. Everybody's like,
"Yeah, yeah!" So Lewis Howes and I partner up. If you've never seen Lewis, great guy,
great podcast. I highly recommend you follow everybody I shared, but Lewis is great. So
he's my partner. He's competitive. He played NFL. He's just a great guy.
The part I want to share, and this is exactly how I'd share it if you were in my
mastermind, is everybody had the same exact advantage or disadvantage. We're all
high-achieving entrepreneurs. We're all fishing in the same stream. We're all fishing for
the same trout. We all got the same exact fly rods. We all got the same exact flies that
were on the end, that we were using to catch them. We all got a quick little lesson
before we do it, and there was a guide at each station that we all had access to. So
everything was fair, everything was even.
So they blow the whistle, and we go to our first stand. We go to our first place. We're
fishing, and Lewis and I are fishing and catching nothing. We don't hear anybody
catching anything. So we go to our second station, and there was a couple of bites here,
a couple people yelling. But here's the thing. Because of my obsession of finding what
works, I did something really intelligent. I started seeing what was working and what
doesn't.
Now, I want to back up a little bit. I have to say I had an unfair advantage going in here,
because my grandfather taught me how to fly fish before I was 10. He passed away
when I was 10. He was passionate about it. So I had, like any successful business, that
there was a heart and it was an emotion, it was a feeling attached to fly fishing. I felt like
I was making my grandfather smile in heaven, or wherever you believe people go to. So
that's one thing. Secondly, I'm quietly competitive. I'm not the, "Yeah, I'm going to do
this!" I'm like, "Oh, yeah. Let's do it." Inside, I'm like, "I'm kicking all of your butts," right?

So I had this unfair advantage of being competitive, this unfair advantage of having my
heart attached to it. So that's two things.
Now, we start. Here's what I did. I started noticing that the fish weren't biting. But when
I walk up to the stream and the water was so crystal clear, that if there was trout, they'd
scurry. So they saw me. So by the second or third hole, I started standing back or
crawling up to the stream. And I started catching more fish. Then I realized that where
the water like will come over a rock, that wherever it hits and it churns where it's white,
not when it gets flat, not when it's smooth, not where there's heavy rapids, but this little
flat spot behind the rocks, I notice if I landed my fly there, I'd get a bite right away. So I
started noticing where to land. Now, I'm maybe at the fourth hole. I'm starting to catch
more fish.
I also realized this, that with fly fishing, if you leave the slack out, when the trout hits the
fly, if you have slack and you go to set the hook ... You have slack, you pull on the slack,
you don't set the hook, they spit it out. Now, I just want to say, no fish were hurt. Every
single fish was thrown back, okay? So now I realize that when I land it, I had to keep it
taut, like no slack. So soon as the fish would hit, I could give it a tug, set the hook. Now,
all of a sudden, I'm getting more fish and bigger fish.
I'm watching Lewis, who's my buddy and my teammate, and he's just fishing faster. But
he's like being on a treadmill trying to get someplace, and he was on six, and he turns it
up to eight. He's running faster, but he's not going anywhere. Where I'm doing
something that's so wisdom-filled, shows that I'm so smart. I was figuring out, I'm saying
that sarcastically, I was figuring out what was working, and I was doing more of it.
Then I get to realize that when you land it, it's the most exciting time for the trout. Soon
as the fly hits, they'd hit the fly. And I missed it so many times in the beginning. But by
the time I got to the fifth hole, I knew that I'd stand back. I knew that I'd land the fly in a
certain spot. I knew that I'd keep all the string taut so when the fish hit, I could yank
right away. And I knew soon as it landed was the time to be ready.
At the last hole, I caught 12 different trout, more than anyone had caught all day, and I
caught this beautiful monster trout. And here's what I realized as I was going, I was just
learning obsessively what was working and did more of it. I learned what wasn't
working, and I did less of that. That's what I believe to be is one of my unfair advantages
when it comes to marketing. I don't care if your company is $10 million a year, $10,000
a year, or $100 million a year, in most cases, our marketing departments aren't
obsessively measuring the things that work and obsessively measuring the things that
don't, and either fixing or dropping the things that don't and putting more fuel, putting
more gas in the things that do.
So now, I want to show you how I measure my marketing so you guys can implement it
in your business. Now, that story I told you, I took the time to tell you, 'cause I wanted
to show you the magic of a story. It's like, "Okay, Dean's unique ability, one of his things
is he measures obsessively. He sees things other people don't." Listen, all those amazing
buddies of mine are great entrepreneurs. All of them wanted to win, but a lot of times
